meta.title: 7.3 The Platform-Product Boundary
meta.description: Navigate the Supabase-specific versus generic-database-layer decision using variation confidence, extension potential, and team capacity — and learn when the abstraction argument is strongest versus weakest to resolve boundary debates without deferring to engineering instinct.

blocks.prompt:
The Make Product team wants a feature: "one-click Supabase integration." They ask Platform to build a generic "database integration layer" that supports Supabase, Firebase, PlanetScale, and Neon. Platform says that's a 3-month project. Product says Supabase-only takes 2 weeks. Who's right?

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

By the end of this module, you'll be able to evaluate whether a platform abstraction request is premature or justified using the rule of three, articulate the platform tax of a given abstraction, and give a constructive answer that delivers the specific implementation now while preserving the option to abstract later.

Platform abstractions should follow the rule of three — build specific the first time, note duplication the second, and only design the abstraction when three concrete use cases have revealed exactly which variation axes matter, because a wrong abstraction built on imagined future demand is more expensive than no abstraction at all. It's not a dysfunction — it's inherent to the different mandates. Understanding how to navigate this tension is a core competency for a Platform PM, because the wrong answer in either direction has significant costs.

---

**The Case for the Abstraction**

The platform team's instinct to build a "database integration layer" instead of a Supabase-specific integration is architecturally sound reasoning. If you build Supabase-specific today, and Firebase demand materializes in three months, you build Firebase-specific. Then PlanetScale. By integration number four, you have four separate implementations with no shared infrastructure, divergent error handling, inconsistent authentication patterns, and four times the maintenance burden. The abstraction you avoided building in Week 1 is now a refactor that costs more than the original abstraction would have.

The abstraction argument is strongest when:
- The problem domain has well-understood variation (databases are all roughly similar — connection, query, auth)
- You have confident evidence that multiple instances of the variation will be needed
- The abstraction unlocks third-party extensibility (a generic database integration layer is something partners can extend with their own providers)
- You're early enough that the abstraction doesn't require rewriting existing implementations

---

**The Case Against the Abstraction**

Product's instinct to build Supabase-specific is also sound reasoning. Abstractions are not free. Every abstraction layer adds:

- **Complexity:** The abstraction has to accommodate all current and future database providers. This means negotiating the lowest-common denominator of their capabilities, or building escape hatches for provider-specific features. Both options add complexity.
- **Latency:** More layers means more function calls, more configuration resolution, more interface translation. Not always significant, but real.
- **Maintenance:** The abstraction itself is a codebase that has to be maintained, documented, and updated when a provider's API changes.
- **Risk:** If the abstraction is wrong (you designed for the wrong variation axes), you have to refactor the abstraction *and* all implementations. A wrong abstraction is worse than no abstraction.

The abstraction argument is weakest when:
- You're speculating about future providers without concrete evidence
- The problem domain has more variation than it appears (databases seem similar until you deal with Firebase's document model vs. Postgres's relational model vs. PlanetScale's branching model)
- The platform team is already capacity-constrained and a 3-month abstraction project delays other work that's more impactful
- Supabase is a strategic partnership with Figma and the others are genuinely speculative

blocks.question: A PM argues: 'We know we'll need Firebase and PlanetScale in 6 months — our enterprise customers have all three. That's enough evidence to build the abstraction now.' Is this the rule of three, and how should you evaluate the claim?
blocks.answer: This is not the rule of three. The rule requires three concrete, already-built implementations that have revealed real variation — not three anticipated future requests. The PM's framing is speculative: 'we know we'll need' is a prediction, not evidence. The right response is to build Supabase-specific now, ensuring the implementation has a clean interface that could be extracted into an abstraction later. When Firebase is actually requested and being built, you'll have real variation data from two implementations. When PlanetScale arrives, you'll have three — and at that point you'll know exactly which variation axes matter (e.g., document vs. relational vs. branching model) rather than guessing at them from feature requests.

blocks.content:
---

**The Rule of Three**

The practical heuristic: build it specific the first time. Refactor when the *third* use case arrives.

First use case: Supabase. Build Supabase-specific. Ship in 2 weeks.

Second use case: Firebase. Evaluate whether the Supabase implementation can be extended with moderate effort. If yes, extend it. If not, build Firebase-specific alongside it and note the pattern.

Third use case: PlanetScale. You now have concrete evidence of variation from two real implementations. You know what's common and what's different. Build the abstraction informed by actual use cases, not imagined ones. The abstraction will be better because it's grounded in reality.

This heuristic has a name in software engineering: the "rule of three" — the first time you write something, just write it. The second time, note the duplication. The third time, refactor into an abstraction.

---

**How to Say No Constructively**

Platform PMs often need to decline platform abstraction requests that are premature. The failure mode is refusing without offering a path forward. The constructive version:

"We'll build Supabase-specific now, with a clean interface that we can extract into an abstraction later if the demand materializes. I'll make sure the Supabase implementation doesn't hardcode assumptions that would make extraction painful. If Firebase demand materializes and we hit the third provider, I'll bring a proposal to prioritize the abstraction — at that point we'll have real evidence to justify the investment."

This answer gives Product what they need (Supabase in 2 weeks), keeps the future optionality open (clean interface, extractable later), and sets a clear trigger for when Platform will revisit the abstraction investment (third provider).

---

**The Platform Tax**

Every abstraction has a "platform tax" — the cost in complexity, latency, and developer overhead that users of the abstraction pay compared to a direct implementation. When the platform tax is low and the abstraction saves significant redundant work across multiple teams, it's worth paying. When the platform tax is high and the abstraction solves a problem only one team has, it's not.

The Platform PM's job is to be honest about the platform tax. When a product team is asking for a 3-month abstraction, the Platform PM should be able to articulate: "Here's the platform tax of this abstraction — the added latency, the API surface complexity for each new provider, the documentation burden. Here's the savings if we hit 4 providers. Here's the break-even. We're not at break-even yet."

---

**Real-World Parallels**

**AWS:** S3 was built as a specific internal need at Amazon — a storage service for Amazon.com's product catalog. The abstraction (generic object storage) came from solving the specific problem well. The abstraction was valid because the demand signal was clear.

**Stripe:** Built card payment processing first. Extended to bank transfers, international payments, and eventually a generic payment methods abstraction — after seeing the variation across markets.

**Figma:** Figma's plugin API is an abstraction over Figma's internal data model. It took years of internal stability before Figma could expose it externally with confidence. The plugin API launched in 2019, four years after Figma's founding.

The pattern: specific before abstract, with the abstraction informed by real variation.

---

## How to Think About This

The platform-product boundary debate is really a question about when you have enough evidence to design the right abstraction. The third use case isn't an arbitrary threshold — it's the minimum number of real implementations that reveal genuine variation. With one use case (Supabase), you're guessing at variation axes. With two, you have one data point of contrast. With three, you can see the pattern. Designing the abstraction before you've seen the pattern means encoding your assumptions into infrastructure that's expensive to change — and your assumptions are usually wrong in the details that matter most.

blocks.question: The Make agent currently supports three "tool calls": write file, read file, and run terminal command. The agent team wants to add support for 15 more tools: web search, screenshot, browser automation, API calls, database queries, etc. Should Platform build a generic "tool framework" or implement each tool specifically?
blocks.answer:
*This is a rule-of-three scenario. At 3 tools, specific implementations make sense. At 15, you've crossed the threshold where a generic tool framework (like Anthropic's tool_use spec) is justified. The PM's answer: build the tool framework alongside the first 3-4 new tools, so you have concrete cases to design against.*

---

blocks.title:
Find a product you use that has a public API or integration marketplace. Look at the integrations it offers. Pick three integrations in the same category (e.g., three CRM integrations, three analytics integrations). Do they look like they were built on a shared abstraction, or do they look like three separate implementations? What tells you which?

---
blocks.solution: Signs that integrations were built on a shared abstraction: they share identical authentication patterns (same OAuth flow, same credential field names), they have consistent error handling (same error codes for similar failures), they have similar performance characteristics, and the documentation follows a template with the same fields across all three. Signs that they're separate implementations: the authentication setup differs (one uses OAuth, one uses API keys, one uses a webhook secret), error messages have different formats, some integrations have features that others lack for no obvious reason, and the documentation has different levels of quality. Most real-world integration marketplaces are a mix — the abstraction covers the happy path (connect, sync, disconnect) but breaks down at the edges (provider-specific features, custom field mapping, rate limit handling). This exercise makes the platform tax visible: the lowest-common-denominator abstraction forces every integration to look the same, which means provider-specific power features can't be surfaced. The question for your product is whether that trade — uniformity for simplicity vs. expressiveness for depth — is the right one for your users.

blocks.prompt:
Explain the "rule of three" to a product manager who keeps asking Platform to build abstractions for every new feature. Why isn't the second use case enough evidence to justify the abstraction? What does the third use case reveal that the second doesn't?

---

questions: Think of a time you were involved in building an abstraction too early — before you had enough real use cases to know what variation you were abstracting over. What happened when the third or fourth use case arrived with different characteristics than you'd assumed? What was the refactoring cost?

Recall a situation where the team deferred abstraction too long — where you ended up with four or five specific implementations that should have been generalized after the third. What finally triggered the decision to abstract, and was it harder than it would have been earlier? What signal would have told you the right time?

When you've been on a platform team — or worked closely with one — what was the hardest version of the platform-product boundary tension? Where did the product team's urgency and the platform team's architectural caution genuinely conflict, and how was it resolved? Looking back, who was right?

---

blocks.content:
Platform abstractions should be built when three concrete use cases provide enough variation evidence to design the right abstraction — not speculatively — and the Platform PM's job is to offer a specific implementation with a clean interface now, with a credible trigger for when abstraction investment is justified.

---

blocks.prompt:
You've handled the Make-specific boundary. But Make Platform could serve more than Make — it could be Figma's shared runtime for interactive experiences across Design, FigJam, and Sites. Should it? That's the cross-product leverage question.

---

reviewQuestions.question: What is the primary purpose of the 'rule of three' in platform abstraction decisions?
options: To require sign-off from three engineering leads before any new platform abstraction is approved
To ensure abstractions are built only after three concrete use cases have revealed which variation axes actually matter
To limit the number of platform abstractions a single team can own at once
reviewQuestions.explanation: The rule of three says: build specific the first time, note duplication the second, and build the abstraction the third time when real variation across multiple implementations reveals the correct design axes. A wrong abstraction built on imagined variation is more expensive to fix than no abstraction at all.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Well done! Three real use cases give you enough variation data to design the abstraction correctly — not speculatively.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The rule of three isn't a governance approval process or a team capacity limit. It's a timing heuristic: don't design abstractions until you've seen enough real variation to know which axes matter. One or two use cases aren't enough — the third is what reveals the pattern.

reviewQuestions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the 'platform tax' of an abstraction?
options: The cost in complexity, latency, and developer overhead that users of the abstraction pay compared to a direct implementation
The engineering time required to build the abstraction layer itself
The maintenance fee charged to product teams that consume shared platform infrastructure
reviewQuestions.explanation: Platform tax is the ongoing cost paid by every consumer of the abstraction: more complexity because the abstraction must accommodate all providers, more latency from additional function call layers, and more developer overhead from the abstraction's own interface. When this tax exceeds the savings from avoiding duplicated implementations, the abstraction isn't justified.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's it! Platform tax is the downstream cost absorbed by every user of the abstraction — not just the one-time build cost.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The build time and any notional fees are one-time costs. Platform tax refers to the ongoing costs that every consumer pays: the complexity of a lowest-common-denominator API, the latency of extra abstraction layers, and the documentation burden of a more complex interface.

reviewQuestions.question: A product team requests a Supabase integration. Platform proposes building a generic database abstraction layer covering four providers. Which response is correct under the rule of three?
options: Build the generic abstraction now — it's better architecture and will save time when Firebase is requested
Defer both until enterprise customers confirm demand for all four providers in writing
Build Supabase-specific now with a clean interface, and revisit abstraction when a third database provider is concretely needed
reviewQuestions.explanation: The rule of three says build specific the first time. With only one provider (Supabase), the variation axes across database types aren't yet known. The right answer is a Supabase-specific implementation with a clean, extractable interface — preserving the option to abstract later when real variation data from two or three implementations reveals what the abstraction needs to handle.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! Clean interface now, abstraction when you have the evidence. That's the rule of three in practice.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Deferring entirely and building a pre-emptive abstraction are both wrong. The rule of three points to a middle path: build the specific thing now (2 weeks), keep the interface clean so it can be extracted later, and trigger the abstraction conversation only when you have real variation data from multiple concrete implementations.
